record.field: Tools to read, list, and query Odoo models and records
Tools to create, update, delete, and execute methods on database records
Tools to inspect and search custom module and addons code
High-risk tools including safe_eval scripting, EChart visualizations, module compilers, and subagent queries

Discover installed Odoo models in the database. Use when the user asks: "what models exist", "which Odoo models are available", "show me all models", or before using search_read on an unfamiliar model. Supports regex filter on technical names.

Retrieve the FIELD definitions of an Odoo model (field names, types, relations). Call this ONCE per model when you are not 100% certain of field names. After calling it, immediately proceed to search_read or execute_orm — do NOT call get_model_schema again on the same model. IMPORTANT: This tool shows FIELDS only, NOT Python methods. To call a business method use execute_orm directly — common methods you already know: action_confirm, action_quotations_send, action_cancel, write, unlink. Never read source code files to look up method names.

Find, list, filter, or count records in any Odoo model. ALWAYS call this tool when the user asks: "show me all", "find customers", "list invoices", "get orders", "how many records", "search for", "fetch", "retrieve". Never answer data questions from memory — always query with this tool first. IMPORTANT: Never guess field names. If you are not 100% certain which fields exist on the model, call get_model_schema first, then use the exact field names it returns. Domain format: [['field','operator',value]]. Use 'ilike' for fuzzy name search, '=' for exact match. Example: [['state','=','draft'],['partner_id.name','ilike','Azure']].

Get full details of a single record by its database ID. Use when the user says: "show me record #X", "get details of ID", "open record", or after search_read returns an ID and the user wants full field details.

Aggregate and group database records for statistics and reports. Use when the user asks: "total sales by month", "count orders per customer", "sum revenue by product", "how much in total", "group by", "breakdown of", "average", "statistics". Never calculate aggregates manually from raw records — use this tool. Call directly without schema lookup — you know the field names for standard Odoo models.

Create a new record in Odoo. Use when the user says: "create a", "add a new", "make a new", "register a", "insert a". Examples: create a customer, create a sale order, add a new product. You know the required fields for common models — call this directly without schema lookup.

Update fields on one or more existing records. Use when the user says: "update", "change", "set the", "modify", "edit record", "rename". Always use search_read first to get the record IDs before updating.
Permanently delete records from Odoo. Use when the user says: "delete", "remove", "unlink", "erase". Always confirm record IDs with search_read before deleting. This action is irreversible.

Run an Odoo workflow action or business method on records. Use when the user says: "confirm the order", "validate the invoice", "approve", "cancel", "post", "mark as done", "send", "reset to draft". Trigger: any workflow state change that isn't just a field write.

Search the module source code for classes, methods, or patterns. Use when the user asks: "where is X defined", "find the class", "search codebase for", "which file contains", "show me the implementation of". Returns file path, line number, and matching content. IMPORTANT: Do NOT use this to discover method names for standard Odoo models (sale.order, account.move, etc.) — call execute_orm directly with the method name you already know.

Read the contents of a source file from the module directory. Use when the user says: "show me the file", "read the code in", "open the file", "what is in", or after code_search returns a file path and you need to see the full content.

Execute a Python script in Odoo's safe_eval sandbox with full ORM access. Use for complex multi-step operations that no single tool covers. NEVER write "import" or "from" statements — all modules are pre-loaded: base64, io, BytesIO, StringIO, xlrd, openpyxl, re, json, datetime, date, timedelta, math, itertools, defaultdict, Counter, print. Do NOT use python "with" statements (they are forbidden by Odoo safe_eval; write explicit assignments and close manually instead). IMPORTANT: Use "return" to expose results to the AI — print() output goes to server logs only and will NOT be visible in the response. To read a spreadsheet attachment use the built-in helper: read_excel(attachment_id) → list of row lists (handles .xls, .xlsx, AND .ods automatically). Note: attachment.datas is base64-encoded; attachment.raw is raw bytes — read_excel handles the decode internally. Valid ir.attachment fields: id, name, mimetype, file_size, datas, raw, url, res_model, res_id (datas_fname was removed in Odoo 13, do not use it).

Post a message or internal note to a record's chatter (mail.thread). Use when user says: "add a note to", "log a message on", "comment on", "send internal note to", "notify about", "leave a comment on". Parameters: model, record_id, body (text), message_type (comment=email to followers, internal=private note).

List files and images attached to a record. Use when user says: "show attachments on", "what files are on", "get documents for", "list images attached to". Returns file names, types, sizes, and download URLs. Parameters: model, record_id.

Read the content of a specific attachment by ID. Use when user says: "read the file", "show the content of", "what's in this document", "open attachment". Returns metadata + base64 content if under 1MB. Get the attachment_id first from get_attachments. Parameters: attachment_id, include_data (default true).
{"attachment": {"name": "invoice.pdf", "mimetype": "application/pdf"}, "data_included": false, "warnings": ["File is 2MB, over 1MB cap. Use the URL to download."]}
Attach a file (base64 encoded) to a record. Use when user says: "attach file to", "upload document to", "add attachment to". Parameters: model, record_id, filename, mimetype, datas (base64 string).

Write an image or binary field on a record by fetching bytes from a URL. Use when user says: "set product image from URL", "update avatar from", "set logo to", "upload image to field". avatar_128/256/512/1024/1920 all auto-redirect to image_1920. Parameters: model, record_id, field_name (e.g. image_1920), source (http/https URL).

Create multiple records in a single operation (max 1000). Use when user says: "create multiple", "bulk create", "add these N records", "import a list of". More efficient than calling create_record N times. Parameters: model, vals_list (array of value dicts).

Update multiple records with the same values in a single operation (max 1000). Use when user says: "update all", "set field X on these records", "bulk update", "change status of all". Parameters: model, record_ids (array of IDs), values (dict of field→value).
Delete multiple records in a single operation (max 1000). Use when user says: "delete all", "remove these records", "bulk delete", "clean up all X". Parameters: model, record_ids (array of IDs).

Resolve an outdated Odoo model name to its current name. Use when a tool call fails with "model not found", or before using known-renamed models: account.invoice→account.move, hr.holidays→hr.leave, crm.case.categ→crm.tag. Returns current name and rename status. Parameters: model.

Quick AR/AP health check — open invoices, overdue amounts, draft backlog. Use when user says: "what's our AR situation", "how many unpaid invoices", "overdue bills", "accounting overview", "cash flow situation", "how much do customers owe us". No parameters needed.

Import records from a CSV or Excel file that was uploaded via the paperclip button. Use when user uploads a file and says: "import this", "create records from this file", "load products from Excel", "bulk import from spreadsheet". The file must be uploaded first (use the paperclip). Parameters: attachment_id (from the staged file), model (Odoo model to import into), has_header (true if first row = field names, default true). First row must be Odoo field names (e.g. name, email, phone for res.partner).

Create and save an Apache ECharts visualization. Use when user asks: "create a chart", "visualize", "plot", "show me a graph". SINGLE tool call — do NOT call read_group separately first. Write data_code Python using ECharts dataset format: query ORM inside data_code, build source as list-of-lists (first row = headers), return complete options dict. BAR/LINE: result=env['sale.order'].read_group(domain=[['date_order','>=','2026-06-01'],['state','not in',['cancel']]],fields=['amount_total:sum'],groupby=['date_order:day']); source=[['Date','Sales']]+[[str(r.get('date_order:day','')),r.get('amount_total',0)] for r in result]; return {'title':{'text':'June Sales'},'dataset':{'source':source},'xAxis':{'type':'category'},'yAxis':{'type':'value'},'series':[{'type':'bar','name':'Sales'}]}. PIE: result=env['sale.order'].read_group(domain=[],fields=['amount_total:sum'],groupby=['partner_id']); source=[['Customer','Amount']]+[[r['partner_id'][1],r.get('amount_total',0)] for r in result if r.get('partner_id')]; return {'title':{'text':'By Customer'},'dataset':{'source':source},'series':[{'type':'pie','radius':'60%','encode':{'itemName':'Customer','value':'Amount'}}]}. RULES: always use dataset.source, never xAxis.data or series.data, data_code must end with return dict.

Delegate a task to a different AI agent configured in the system. Use when the user says: "ask the other agent", "use the specialist agent", "delegate to", or when the current agent lacks tools the target agent has. Max recursion depth: 3.
